title: Optical Stability and Photophysics of NV Centers in Diamond up to 120 GPa
authors: Kin On Ho, Cassandra Dailledouze, Vytautas Žalandauskas, Grégoire Le Caruyer, Marek Maciaszek, Claire Roussy, Marie-Pierre Adam, Martin Schmidt, Loïc Toraille, Paul Loubeyre, Lukas Razinkovas, Jean-François Roch
abstract: The nitrogen vacancy (NV) center has emerged as a powerful quantum sensor in high-pressure research, with the observation of optically detected magnetic resonance at megabar pressures. However, some aspects of NV physics require further investigation to optimize the development of NV-based sensing under pressure. Here, we study both experimentally and theoretically the optical properties of the NV center under hydrostatic pressure. We investigate the evolution of the zero-phonon line (ZPL) position, radiative lifetimes, optical lineshapes, and photoionization thresholds of the NV center under pressures up to ~120 GPa. We also provide spectroscopic guidelines for performing high-pressure optical experiments. Our results confirm that the NV center remains a robust quantum sensor under extreme hydrostatic pressures, especially for magnetic characterization.
summary: This research thoroughly investigates how the optical properties of NV centers in diamond change under extreme hydrostatic pressures up to 120 GPa. By combining advanced DFT calculations with DAC experiments, the authors demonstrate that the NV center remains a robust quantum sensor. The findings provide critical spectroscopic guidelines for utilizing NV centers in high-pressure quantum sensing applications.
main_problem: To investigate the optical properties and stability of the Nitrogen Vacancy (NV) center in diamond under extreme hydrostatic pressures up to 120 GPa to optimize its use as a quantum sensor.
main_result: The NV center remains a robust quantum sensor under extreme hydrostatic pressures, maintaining stable quantum efficiency for optical readout, despite enhanced electron-phonon coupling.
method: The study combines theoretical calculations using DFT (HSE06, r2SCAN) with experimental measurements performed in a Diamond Anvil Cell (DAC) setup.
model_or_system: The physical system is the negatively charged nitrogen vacancy (NV-) center in diamond. The analysis models its electronic structure, optical transitions (ZPL, PSB), and photoionization pathways under varying hydrostatic pressures.
key_observables: Zero-Phonon Line (ZPL) position shift, radiative lifetimes, optical lineshapes, photoionization thresholds, and electron-phonon coupling parameters (HR factor, $K_2$).
important_parameters: Pressures up to 120 GPa; ZPL shift exceeding 400 meV at 120 GPa.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes near-hydrostatic conditions for comprehensive study, and theoretical modeling uses approximations like adiabatic Huang-Rhys theory for emission.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the pressure dependence of ZPL shifts, electron-phonon coupling parameters, PL/absorption lineshapes, and photoionization thresholds up to 120 GPa.
paper_structure: The paper systematically investigates the pressure dependence of various optical parameters, comparing theoretical predictions (DFT calculations) with experimental results obtained using a DAC setup, culminating in guidelines for high-pressure optical measurements.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it addresses the stability of a solid-state quantum emitter (NV center) under extreme conditions, which is crucial for developing quantum sensors for high-pressure physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) ZPL shift relative to ambient pressure. Black circles: experiment; orange solid line: theory (hydrostatic); orange dashed line: theory with near-hydrostaticity stress (α = 0.95). (b) Pressure-induced evolution of electron–phonon coupling parameters. Blue solid line: total HR factor S (emission); red solid line: HR factor for JT-inactive modes (absorption); red dashed line: squared vibronic coupling constants K2. (c) Pressure-induced evolution of Debye–Waller factors (DWF). Blue solid line: emission (theory); red solid line: absorption (theory); black circles: emission (experimental). (d) Pressure-induced evolution of theoretical radiative lifetime. (e) Theoretical PL and...

FIG. 2. (a) Photoionization of NV−from 3A2 and 3E states leading to 2E and 4A2 of NV0. (b) Pressure evolution of ion- ization thresholds IP(3A2) and IP(3E) alongside with ZPL. (c) Ionization energies relative to ZPL.

FIG. 3. (a) Illustration of the DAC with a micropillar struc- ture on (100)-diamond for reaching near-hydrostatic pres- sures [16]. We silver-coat the tip of the NV-implanted anvil to avoid interference from the top anvil and increase signal collection. (b) Image of DAC1 with three micropillars. (c) Confocal image of DAC2 with a single micropillar.

FIG. 4. Pressure evolution of the experimental NV PL spec- tra. (Left) Measurements from the TP micropillar at 30 K using 532 nm excitation. (Right) Measurements from the MP micropillar at ≈90 K: bottom panel – 532 nm excitation; top panel – 457 nm excitation. Spectra in each panel are verti- cally offset by pressure-dependent scaling factors.

FIG. 5. (a) Comparison between experimental and theoreti- cal PSBs at approximately 10, 20, 40, 60, and 80 GPa. Solid lines and shaded areas represent calculations and experimen- tal results. Dashed lines indicate the ambient-pressure ref- erence. (b) PL photon yield (arbitrary units) as a function of pressure for excitation at 532 nm and 457 nm. Solid and open markers show the corresponding integrated experimen- tal PL intensities, while solid blue and red lines represent the pressure-dependent absorption cross sections (arbitrary units) sampled at 532 and 457 nm from theory.
